1: <header>Comparison of different methods for consideration of multifunctionality of Peruvian dairy cattle in Life Cycle Assessment </header>
Alessandro Gilardino, Isabel Quispe, Maribel Pacheco Karin Bartl
a Peruvian Life Cycle Assessment and Industrial Ecology Network (PELCAN), Department of Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Católica del Perú, 1801 Avenida Universitaria, San Miguel, Lima 32, Peru
b Universidad Nacional de Moquegua, Prolongación Calle Ancash s/n, Moquegua, Peru

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Livestock multifunctionality Dairy system, LCA Allocation Peru|In Andean dairy systems, livestock generates a daily income through the sale of milk and cheese but also fulfils other roles which are crucial for rural livelihoods such as the contribution of protein to local diets, the provision of draught power and organic fertilizer, the reduction of economic risk and the accrual of savings. The objective of this study was to compare different approaches to cope with multifunctionality of livestock in Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). Based on local data from a smallholder dairy production system in the Southern Peruvian Andes environmental impacts of 1 kg energy corrected milk (ECM) were calculated with LCA methodology considering nine livestock functions: production of dairy products (1) and meat (2) for the market, production of milk for family consumption (3) and as food for calves (4), provision of organic fertilizer (5) and draught power (6), role as savings asset (7), risk insurance (8) and part of family tradition (9). Impacts were distributed among these functions using different methodological approaches: NA, no allocation; EM, economic allocation to market products; ET, economic allocation to all livestock functions; FP, allocation according to farmers per- ception and SE, system expansion (substitution). The results varied considerably depending on the method used. 1 kg of ECM contributed to global warming with 1.65, 1.38, 1.25, 0.71 and 0.89 kg CO2-equivalents using mehods NA, EM, ET, FP and SE, respectively. Similar patterns were observed for acidification and eutrophication potentials. Enteric fermentation was the main source of greenhouse gases whereas eutrophication and acid- ification potentials were determined by alfalfa pasture management. The results of the study showed that: (i) LCA results are highly sensitive to the number and types of livestock functions considered and the allocation criteria applied, (ii) that the economic value of livestock functions does not necessarily reflect their importance for rural livelihoods and (iii) that the application of system expansion/substitution to livestock systems is limited due to lacking equivalency of functions and products from alternative production systems.
2: Milk production in Peru has been growing fast during the last years, with an average yearly increase of 5.77% (Instituto Nacional de Estadística e Informática (INEI), 2017). Although most of this growth has taken place at the coast, milk production is of high importance especially in the Peruvian Andes, due to the lack of other agricultural options and the generation of a daily income for smallholder families through sales of milk and cheese. For low income smallholder produ- cers, livestock is not only a means for income generation but can fulfil other functions such as the contribution of high quality protein to local diets, the provision of draught power and organic fertilizer, the re- duction of economic risk and the accrual of savings to manage cash needs (Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), 2009; 2011). Despite

these positive contributions of livestock to rural livelihoods, livestock products can have significant environmental burdens. Livestock is re- sponsible for about 14.5% of the global annual greenhouse gas emis- sions, whereof milk production contributes with 20% (Gerber et al., 2013). Milk production is also associated with other environmental impacts such as soil degradation and air and water pollution (Rojas- Downing et al., 2017). A Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is a compilation and evaluation of the inputs, outputs and the potential environmental impacts of a pro- duct or a service throughout its life cycle (International Standardization Organization (ISO), 2006). LCA therefore analyses the environmental burden of products and services at all stages of their life cycle, from the extraction of resources, through the production of materials and the product itself to the management of the waste generated. The

environmental burdens of the production system are related to the outputs of the system which are also called the functions of the system. These functions can be goods and services (tangible as well as in- tangible) (International Standardization Organization (ISO), 2006). If a process in LCA delivers several goods and/or services, it is called "multifunctional" (European Commission Joint Research Centre In- stitute for Environment and Sustainability, 2010). One limitation of the LCA methodology is the handling of these multifunctional systems be- cause if a process produces more than one useful output, it is necessary to divide or partition input and output flows of the process among all useful outputs (Sauer, 2012). Multifunctionality can either be handled with impact allocation or system expansion. Impact allocation aims at distributing impacts between products and co-products in a way that reflects the underlying relationships between them (International Standardization Organization (ISO), 2006). The objective of system expansion is to estimate de environmental impacts of the co-products and functions delivered and subtract these impacts from the overall system's impact. System expansion is relevant for consequential LCAs, it's relevancy for attributional questions is limited. Nevertheless, in attributional studies it is generally considered commensurate with substitution and can be used as a basis for allocation (Pelletier et al., 2015).

Most LCA studies of dairy products distribute all emissions to the edible outputs of production, using economic or physical allocation criteria (Chobtang et al., 2017; Rice et al., 2017; Woldegebriel et al., 2017). Only few studies use system expansion for dairy systems (Marton et al., 2016; Chobtang et al., 2016; Zehetmeier et al., 2014). In general, all studies which compare different methods for the handling of co-products in dairy systems show a large fluctuation of the impacts related to milk according to the methods used. Flysjö et al. (2011) as- sessed 4 different allocation criteria (physical, economic, protein and mass) for environmental impact allocated to 1 kg of energy corrected milk (ECM) and showed that carbon footprint of milk is between 32% to 55% smaller when using system expansion (substitution) instead of impact allocation. Kristensen et al. (2011) developed an allocation method based on a multivariate regression model with the two vari- ables annual kg of ECM and annual kg of meat, allocating 14% of the environmental burdens to meat. Nevertheless, in the same study when using system expansion (substitution), 22% of impacts were attributed to meat. O'Brien et al. (2014) considered an allocation method which assigned emissions caused by surplus calves, finished cull cows and dairy females with less than 24 months of age to meat and the re- maining emissions to milk. This approach reduced the impacts of milk between 17% and 3%, compared to other allocation procedures con- sidered in the study. Nevertheless, when using system expansion (sub- stitution), impacts related to milk production were 31% to 40% lower than impacts calculated with allocation. Kiefer et al. (2015) calculated carbon footprint of milk from German grassland-based dairy systems and showed a 10% decrease of impacts assigned to milk when including ecosystem services in the allocation method. System expansion (sub- stitution) resulted in impacts 56% smaller than with conventional economic allocation. Mackenzie et al. (2017) reviewed biophysical al- location methods but concluded that the way these methods are used in LCA does not represent causal physical mechanisms in the dairy pro- duction systems. A few LCA studies exist which focus on the multi- functionality in smallholder dairy systems. Weiler et al. (2014) esti- mated environmental impacts of a smallholder livestock system in Kenya with LCA, considering allocation methods which not only ac- count for production of milk and meat as functions of the cattle, but also include the production of organic fertilizers and the importance of cattle as means of insurance and finance. An inclusion of these non- tangible livestock functions reduced the carbon footprint of milk by 45% compared to a scenario which considered only edible products as system output. Garg et al. (2016) included the products milk and manure as well as the role of cattle as finance and insurance in an LCA assessing the carbon footprint of milk from smallholder systems in

India, and showed that the carbon footprint under consideration of additional functions was by 21% smaller compared to a 100% alloca- tion of impacts to milk. Since the majority of rural Andean households in Peru rely on livelihood strategies based largely upon livestock (Kristjanson et al., 2007), a sound representation of these systems in a LCA is of overriding importance. LCA is commonly used to compare products produced in different regions or with different technologies from an environmental point of view. This use as decision support tool demands for the inclusion of non-food outputs of livestock production for estimation of environmental impacts, in order to avoid misleading conclusions.

The objective of this study is to assess and compare different methods to cope with multifunctionality of livestock in smallholder dairy systems in the Peruvian Andes. Special focus will be laid on the identification and representation of the non-market livestock functions in these systems.

This use as decision support tool demands for the inclusion of non-food outputs of livestock production for estimation of environmental impacts, in order to avoid misleading conclusions.

The objective of this study is to assess and compare different methods to cope with multifunctionality of livestock in smallholder dairy systems in the Peruvian Andes. Special focus will be laid on the identification and representation of the non-market livestock functions in these systems.
7: The study area is the lower part of the district of Torata in Southern Peru, situated between 2000 and 2500 meters above sea level in the province of Mariscal-Nieto, region of Moquegua (Figure1). Torata's climate is characterized by a pronounced seasonality with a rainy season from January to March (monthly average precipitation, 3.67 mm; monthly average temperature, 20°C) and a dry season from April to December (monthly average precipitation, 0.048 mm; average tem- perature, 18°C) (climate station Moquegua; 1450 m.a.s.l.; year 2018,
Fig. 1. Location of the study area in Peru.
Servicio Nacional de Meteorología e Hidrología del Perú (SENAMHI), 2018).

Although, in this region, the agriculture sector in the year 2016 only contributed with 2% to the regional gross value added (Instituto Nacional de Estadística e Informática (INEI), 2017) it employed 17% of the local population (Gobierno Regional de Moquegua, 2013). The most important crops in 2016 have been alfalfa, accounting for 43% of the district's agricultural surface, followed by oregano (5.98%), avocado (4%), apricots (1.38%) and maize (1.11%) (Gobierno Regional de Moquegua, 2017). Alfalfa is the principal input for livestock production in the region. About 99% of milk produced in the Moquegua region is from extensively managed family systems based on alfalfa grazing, the remaining 1% originate from specialized dairy units (information for the year 2015 provided by the Regional Bureau for Agriculture of Moquegua). Average milk yield is low with 7.5 kg/cow and day (Gobierno Regional de Moquegua, 2017) and according to the Regional Bureau for Agriculture 58% of production is sold to industrialized dairy processing facilities, 19% is sold at the local market, 7% is converted into artisanal cheese and 6% is for auto-consumption.

9: The functional unit (FU) considered for the study is one kilogram of energy corrected milk (ECM). ECM (kg) was calculated according to Agroscope Liebefeld-Posieux (2006) as milk (kg) X [0.038 X fat (g/kg) + 153 .024 X protein (g/kg) 0.017 lactose (g/kg)]/3.14.
10: The local milk production system is the core system and was mod- elled from cradle to farm gate taking into account all main processes of this system and its main production inputs. The whole dairy herd, in- cluding lactating and dry cows, heifers, calves and bulls was considered to be the biological production unit. Non lactating animals of the dairy herd are an essential part of the system as bulls are used for in- semination and labor, female calves and heifers are reared in order to replace old dairy cows and male calves and young bulls constitute a co- product of the system being sold at a certain age as surplus animals. Furthermore the production of local feeds considering fertilizers, irri- gation, pesticides and machinery and non-local feeds with related production and transport processes were considered. The production of ancillary inputs such as fuels and electricity as well as drinking water for the cattle was included as well. Figure 2 (graph a) shows the system boundaries considered for the study.

The equipment of the farms with roads, irrigation infrastructure, buildings and tools was excluded as a very small impact can be ex- pected due to very basic infrastructure such as unpaved roads, un- fortified irrigation channels with an irrigation system based on gravity, and the lack of specialized tools for livestock keeping (tools used are axe, shovel, rope, bucket, etc.). Furthermore, medicines and seeds were excluded due to the lack appropriate unit processes in LCA databases. Carbon sequestration and biogenic carbon emissions of locally pro- duced feeds such as alfalfa and maize were not taken into account due to vegetation cycles shorter than one year (Kristensen et al., 2015).

12: Data for the core system was collected by means of single structured interviews with farmers in the study area. A total of 50 farmers have been randomly selected and surveyed. Special attention was paid to an even distribution of the sample farms over the study area. Further se- lection criteria were the possession of at least one dairy cow, a non- industrial and family-run production system and finally the willingness of the farmers to participate in the process. The sample size has been reduced to 34 after eliminating interviews lacking relevant information. The final sample included approximately 24% of producing cows in the Torata district (111 out of a total of 454 producing cows;

Gobierno Regional de Moquegua, 2017). Information recorded included herd structure and management, crop management, production levels, destiny and prices of products and co-products as well as origin and prices of production inputs. Variables required for the quantification of matter and energy flows, which could not be obtained by means of the survey, have been identified based on information provided by local experts (average body weights, use of pesticides and veterinary pro- ducts) and literature (dry matter and protein contents, yield and di- gestibility for livestock feeds from Bartl et al., 2009; National Research Council (NRC), 2001; Instituto Nacional de Innovación Agraria (INIA), 2010; Gobierno Regional de Moquegua; 2017). Fat, protein and lactose contents of milk were taken from Torata Municipal Government (2015). Furthermore the farmers were asked to identify the different functions livestock fulfills on their farm. Nine functions were identified: pro- duction of milk (1), cheese (2) and meat (3) for the market; production of milk for family consumption (4); production of manure as fertilizer (5); livestock as savings asset (6) and risk insurance (7); livestock as workforce (8) and tradition (9). Tradition is not a function of livestock but many farmers indicated that they value livestock as part of their family tradition. Production of milk as food for calves has not been identified as a livestock function by farmers but was added to the list by the authors as function number (10) as results of the survey indicated that a certain amount of milk is consumed by the calves during the first 2 months of their live.

A second round of interviews with 19 farmers from the study area was added in order to corroborate data from the first interviews, re- collect missing information and value the livestock functions identified in the first interviews according to farmer's perception. For this purpose the functions were grouped accordingly: group 1, production of market goods milk, cheese and meat (functions 1, 2 and 3); group 2, production of edible products for family consumption (function 4); group 3, pro- duction of manure as fertilizer (function 5); group 4, livestock as sav- ings asset and risk insurance (functions 6 and 7); group 5, livestock as workforce (function 8); group 6, tradition (function 9). Production of milk as food for calves (function number 10) has not been included in this task as this function has not been identified by farmers. A two-entry weighting table was applied where farmers had to compare each one of the six groups with all the others and determine the priority function.

For the system expansion/substitution approach, data about typical cattle fattening operations in Puno was collected, in order to obtain the information necessary to model the environmental impacts of meat from an alternative system. The region of Puno is situated in the nearby highlands and most of the meat consumed in Moquegua is produced in this region. Structured interviews were conducted with 3 technicians from the Ministry of Agriculture specialized in cattle fattening and additionally 7 farmers were surveyed to quantify inputs and outputs of production processes. Information recorded included herd structure and management, crop management, feed consumption and production le- vels.

16: In the current study different allocation methods as well as system expansion/substitution have been used in order to cope with the multi- functionality of the livestock system. Four different allocation methods were utilized, each of them characterized by a set of livestock functions (see figure 2, graphs b to e). In the following list, the numbers in brackets indicate the ID of each function according to chapter 2.2.3.:
No allocation: 100% of environmental burdens were attributed to total milk produced.

Economic market: Environmental burdens were allocated to the market goods milk (sold as fresh milk or as cheese) (1 and 2) and meat (3) according to their contribution to farm income. The market prices were obtained from interviews with local farmers and were 0.337 US$ per liter of milk, 3.35 US$ per kilogram of cheese and 547, 164, 650 and 312 US$ for one cow, calve, bull and growing male (15 to 36 months), respectively.

0: Fig. 2. System boundary for the core production system (a) and different allocation scenarios (b to e) Footnote to Fig. 2: Milk for sale includes fresh milk and cheese.

Economic total: Environmental burdens were allocated to market (1, 2 and 3) and non-market functions according to their economic value. The economic value of the function "production of milk for family consumption (4)" was assumed to be the local market price of one liter of processed commercial milk which is 0.95 US$. The monetary value of the function "production of manure as fertilizer (5)" was based on the price of N in urea (46% of N content) which equals 1.01 US$ per kilogram N. The use of cattle as savings asset (6) guarantees farmers' liquidity in the face of cash needs. It's economic value was estimated to equal the amount of annual interest payment for a loan the size of the economic value of the animals sold annually. The interest rate in the bank with highest market share in the region is 16%, which accounts

for 152 US$ annually (Banco de Crédito del Perú, 2016). The economic value of the function "risk insurance (7)" was calculated as the yearly payment for the agricultural health care plan provided by the Peruvian government, which costs 127.5 US$ annually (Seguro Social de Salud (ESSALUD), 2016). The economic value of the function "livestock as workforce" (8) was assumed to be identical to the cost of renting a tractor in order to plough and harrow the size of land occupied by fodder crops and annually worked with a labor animal. One tractor hour was considered to cost 4.06 US$. Finally, the economic value of the function "production of milk as food for calves (10)" was estimated to be the price of a locally available milk substitute for calves, costing 0.0125 US$ per liter (Battilana, 2016). 2.2.4.1. System expansion/substitution. In this approach, the system was expanded in order to include alternative production systems for the co- products meat, milk for family consumption, milk as food for calves, manure and workforce. Although the system expansion approach in general is not applicable to attributional LCA, we followed the methodology of other studies (Flysjö et al., 2011; Garg et al., 2016) using it to determine impacts avoided by the system studied (substitution approach, figure 3). The functions of cattle as savings asset and risk insurance were not considered in this approach due to the lack of adequate unit processes in LCA databases.

The alternative systems selected provide products and functions available in the study region and with outputs that are functionally equal to the outputs of the system studied. It was assumed that all sold animals (cows, calves, bulls and growing males) replace beef from an alternative production system. This alternative system was considered to be a typical smallholder fattening system from the highlands in the department of Puno, characterized by an average fattening period of 109 days and nutrition based on oat hay and concentrate, the first one produced locally with urea fertilizer as exclusive non-local input. Bulls were bought from local multi-purpose systems with 2 years of age and 450 kg average body weight and sold with 550 kg average body weight. Milk for family consumption was considered to replace commercial milk from an international dairy company in Moquegua. According to a study of the local dairy market of Moquegua (Centro Peruano de Estudios Sociales, 2017), it was assumed that consumers buy 63% of their total annual milk purchase as whole ultra-pasteurized fresh milk in tetrapak containers and 36% as evaporated milk in cans. Milk as food for calves was assumed to replace a locally available milk substitute for

Economic total: Environmental burdens were allocated to market (1, 2 and 3) and non-market functions according to their economic value. The economic value of the function "production of milk for family consumption (4)" was assumed to be the local market price of one liter of processed commercial milk which is 0.95 US$. The monetary value of the function "production of manure as fertilizer (5)" was based on the price of N in urea (46% of N content) which equals 1.01 US$ per kilogram N. The use of cattle as savings asset (6) guarantees farmers' liquidity in the face of cash needs. It's economic value was estimated to equal the amount of annual interest payment for a loan the size of the economic value of the animals sold annually. The interest rate in the bank with highest market share in the region is 16%, which accounts

for 152 US$ annually (Banco de Crédito del Perú, 2016). The economic value of the function "risk insurance (7)" was calculated as the yearly payment for the agricultural health care plan provided by the Peruvian government, which costs 127.5 US$ annually (Seguro Social de Salud (ESSALUD), 2016). The economic value of the function "livestock as workforce" (8) was assumed to be identical to the cost of renting a tractor in order to plough and harrow the size of land occupied by fodder crops and annually worked with a labor animal. One tractor hour was considered to cost 4.06 US$. Finally, the economic value of the function "production of milk as food for calves (10)" was estimated to be the price of a locally available milk substitute for calves, costing 0.0125 US$ per liter (Battilana, 2016). 9: Regional data was taken for processes in the core system whenever available. The average production system was modelled based on the results of the farmer survey in the study region (table 2). Annual pro- duction levels have been calculated assuming a stable herd structure throughout the year and the dry period was accounted for when cal- culating the annual average of daily milk production.

Unit processes for background data were taken from the ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016) with the exception of milk substitute, which was taken from the Agri-footprint database (Agri-foot- print, 2018). Special attention was paid to a clear understanding and sound representation of livestock nutrition because both, the modelling of emissions from livestock and manure, as well as the land surface required for feed production depend on the quality and quantity of the diet consumed. Due to the typical livestock management system based on restricted grazing, where cows are tied up to a stick and allowed to graze an area restricted by the length of the rope, no information about the exact amount of forage ingested was available. Therefore the amount of forage ingested had to be estimated assuming an average dry matter intake and protein requirements for small breed cows according to National Research Council (NRC, 2001) and using collected data

such as milk production, body weight estimates and supplement intake (maize straw and concentrate) and literature values for food quality (contents of dry matter, protein, yield and digestibility; see chapter 2.2.3.). It was assumed that all protein not covered by supplements must be covered by alfalfa.

Concentrate was modelled in order to reflect nutritional values of a typical regional commercial concentrate with the following ingredients: Wheat middling (25%, produced in Canada), wheat flour (27%, pro- duced in Canada), ground maize grains (31%, produced in Peru), ground barley grains (10%, produced in Argentine), soy cake (7%, produced in Bolivia). Production and transport processes were taken from the ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016).

Local emissions to the atmosphere considered included methane (CH4) emissions from enteric fermentation and manure, direct and in- direct nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions, nitric oxide (NOx) emissions from mineral and organic fertilizers, CO2 emissions from urea fertilization and mineral and biogenic carbon losses due to land use change (LUC) and ammonia (NH3) emissions from urea, urine and dung. Emissions of greenhouse gases (CH4, N20, NOx, CO2) from agricultural activities and livestock were estimated based on the Tier 2 approach according to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) 2006 guidelines (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 2006). For the calculation of carbon emissions due to LUC only the area that within the last 20 years has been converted from natural pasture to cropland (maize or alfalfa) has been considered. This decision is based on IPCC (2006) manual where 20 years is defined to be the time period assumed for carbon stocks to come to equilibrium for the purposes of calculating default coefficients. Ammonia emissions from urea, urine and dung were calculated according to Nemecek (2013). Nitric oxide emissions were calculated with a mass balance approach as the difference be- tween the total amount of N volatilized, in the form of ammonia and N2O, from mineral fertilizers, urine and dung (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 2006) and the NH3-N fraction (Nemecek, 2013). Emissions to water included soluble phosphate (PO4) leached to ground water, run-off of P to surface water and P emissions through erosion of particulate phosphorous to surface water and were calculated according to Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011). Mineral ferti- lizers and manure were considered as sources for heavy metal emissions into ground- and surface water through leaching, into surface water through erosion and to agricultural soils. Emissions were estimated according to Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011), using regional soil erosion data from Rosas (2016). All pesticides applied for crop production were assumed to end up as emissions to the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011). Nitrate emissions to groundwater have not been in- cluded due to lack of data for emission calculations.

The impacts of commercial milk were calculated taking into account emissions from milk powder production in Bolivia, aluminum produc- tion for cans, production of aluminum, polyethylene and cardboard for tetra-packs as well as transport processes and milk production. All unit processes were taken from the ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016) with the exception of milk, which was assumed to be produced in the same system as subject to this study, applying economic total al- location.

To model the alternative meat production system in Puno, back- ground data was taken from ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016). Dry matter, digestibility, energy and protein content of feeds consumed by livestock were retrieved from National Research Council (NRC, 2001) and body weight for growing bulls and food requirements were retrieved from Quispe et al. (2012). Emissions from this system were calculated with the same methods as applied for the core system. As the fattening system represents only a part of the animals life cycle, it was assumed that the bulls, before entering the fattening system, were reared until the age of two years in a typical smallholder system in the Peruvian highlands (all data taken from Bartl et al. (2011) and recalculated with IPCC method 2013 and for a 100-year time horizon).

Emissions of greenhouse gases (CH4, N20, NOx, CO2) from agricultural activities and livestock were estimated based on the Tier 2 approach according to the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) 2006 guidelines (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 2006). For the calculation of carbon emissions due to LUC only the area that within the last 20 years has been converted from natural pasture to cropland (maize or alfalfa) has been considered. This decision is based on IPCC (2006) manual where 20 years is defined to be the time period assumed for carbon stocks to come to equilibrium for the purposes of calculating default coefficients. Ammonia emissions from urea, urine and dung were calculated according to Nemecek (2013). Nitric oxide emissions were calculated with a mass balance approach as the difference be- tween the total amount of N volatilized, in the form of ammonia and N2O, from mineral fertilizers, urine and dung (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 2006) and the NH3-N fraction (Nemecek, 2013). Emissions to water included soluble phosphate (PO4) leached to ground water, run-off of P to surface water and P emissions through erosion of particulate phosphorous to surface water and were calculated according to Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011). Mineral ferti- lizers and manure were considered as sources for heavy metal emissions into ground- and surface water through leaching, into surface water through erosion and to agricultural soils. Emissions were estimated according to Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011), using regional soil erosion data from Rosas (2016). All pesticides applied for crop production were assumed to end up as emissions to the soil (Nemecek and Schnetzer, 2011). Nitrate emissions to groundwater have not been in- cluded due to lack of data for emission calculations.

The impacts of commercial milk were calculated taking into account emissions from milk powder production in Bolivia, aluminum produc- tion for cans, production of aluminum, polyethylene and cardboard for tetra-packs as well as transport processes and milk production. All unit processes were taken from the ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016) with the exception of milk, which was assumed to be produced in the same system as subject to this study, applying economic total al- location.

To model the alternative meat production system in Puno, back- ground data was taken from ecoinvent database 3.4 (Wernet et al., 2016). Dry matter, digestibility, energy and protein content of feeds consumed by livestock were retrieved from National Research Council (NRC, 2001) and body weight for growing bulls and food requirements were retrieved from Quispe et al. (2012). Emissions from this system were calculated with the same methods as applied for the core system. As the fattening system represents only a part of the animals life cycle, it was assumed that the bulls, before entering the fattening system, were reared until the age of two years in a typical smallholder system in the Peruvian highlands (all data taken from Bartl et al. (2011) and recalculated with IPCC method 2013 and for a 100-year time horizon).
12: Regional data was taken for processes in the core system whenever available. The average production system was modelled based on the results of the farmer survey in the study region (table 2). Annual pro- duction levels have been calculated assuming a stable herd structure throughout the year and the dry period was accounted for when cal- culating the annual average of daily milk production.

15: Global warming, eutrophication and acidification potentials were calculated. For global warming potential, climate change factors of IPCC 2013 with a timeframe of 100 years were used (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 2013). The impact categories acid- ification and eutrophication were calculated with the RECIPE method (version 2016, midpoint method, hierarchist version; Huijbregts et al., 2016). Toxicity was not included as some of the pesticides used were not available in standard LCA-databases. Nevertheless, toxicity impacts can be assumed to be small due to a generally very low level of pro- duction inputs. SimaPro 8.4.0 LCA software (Pré Consultants, 2017) was used for calculations.
16: The sensitivity of the results to LCIA method and to crucial input data was analyzed. The alternative environmental impact assessment method was CML (Guinée et al., 2002). The input with the lowest level of certainty was the amount of alfalfa consumed. Thus the sensitivity of the results to a variation of +/-6.5% in alfalfa consumption was as- sessed.
17: Impacts have been calculated for 1 kg ECM using different alloca- tion approaches and system expansion/substitution (see figure 4).
18: When no allocation is considered, global warming potential of 1 kg ECM is 1.65 kg CO2-quivalents. Enteric fermentation is responsible for 70.95% of the environmental impact, followed by pasture production with 24.70%. 80.60% of total greenhouse gas emissions from pasture are caused by manure dropped on alfalfa pastures during grazing. Supplemental feeds such as maize straw and concentrate contribute only with 4.35% to milk's total global warming potential. In general, 95.58% of impacts have emission sources located within the core system boundaries. Comparing the contribution of different greenhouse gases (GHG), the results show that main impacts can be contributed to methane and dinitrogen monoxide (72.12% and 21.87% of total GHG emissions, respectively). However, by introducing different allocation methods for the assignment of environmental burdens to milk and other co-products or functions from cattle, environmental impacts of 1 kg ECM reduce by 16.1% (Economic market allocation), 24.0% (Economic total allocation) and 56.8% (Farmer's perception allocation) compared to the no allocation scenario.

The system expansion/substitution approach resulted in a global warming potential of 0.89 kg CO2 equivalents/kg ECM. Table 3 pre- sents the emission factors for the alternative production systems con- sidered. From the total annual impact avoided, which accounts for 688 kg CO2-equivalents, 69.5% are avoided due to co-production of beef, 14.9% due to the family consumption of milk, 13.1% due to the use of manure of fertilizer, 2.39% due to the consumption of milk by the calves and 0.19% due to the use of livestock as workforce.

20: Acidification potential of 1 kg ECM without allocation is 9.17 g SO2- equivalents. Impacts caused by alfalfa pasture management account for 92.70% of the total environmental burden. These emissions from alfalfa are mainly Ammonia (94.8%) and Nitrogen oxides (4.69%) caused by livestock manure and urine deposited on the pasture. Furthermore, impacts with emission sources located within the core system boundary account for 92.95% of total acidification potential. Economic market, economic total and farmers' perception allocation methods reduce the
environmental burden of 1 kg ECM by 25.8%, 34.7% and 58.1%, re- spectively, compared to the no allocation scenario.

In addition, the system expansion/substitution approach reduces acidification potential of 1 kg ECM by 50.6%, compared to the no al- location approach. Acidification potential is mainly avoided due to the co-products and functions beef (50.8% of avoided impact), milk for family consumption (23.0% of avoided impact), manure as fertilizer (13.2% of avoided impact) and milk consumed by calves (12.2% of avoided impact) (see table 3).
21: Eutrophication potential for 1 kg ECM is 2.31 g P-equivalents when all environmental burdens are allocated to milk. The main contributor to eutrophication is the alfalfa pasture (96.1%) but emissions from pasture can be traced to manure and urine deposited by livestock. When different allocation methods were applied, eutrophication potential reduced by 25.5% (Economic market), 43.7% (Economic total) and 63.8% (Farmer's perception) compared to the no-allocation scenario.

System expansion/substitution reduced eutrophication potential assigned to milk by 51.9%, relative to the no-allocation scenario. About 92.8% of this reduction is caused by the replacement of beef from fattening systems by the co-product beef from the system studied.
22: The sensitivity of the results to a variation of alfalfa intake in the range of + 6.5% was tested. Milk production responded to this varia- tion as well as nitrogen excretion and methane emissions. All other production parameters, including concentrate and supplement inputs, were assumed to remain unchanged. A linear relationship between al- falfa ingestion and milk production was assumed, although this does not necessarily reflect the real physiological response of the animal to a change in nutrition level. Results showed that an assumed increase of alfalfa ingestion decreases impacts per kg ECM due to a higher pro- duction level, and an assumed decrease of alfalfa ingestion increases impacts per kg ECM. Nevertheless, relative impact reduction due to a higher alfalfa ingestion was smaller than relative impact increase due to a smaller amount of alfalfa ingested (see table 4).

The objective of realizing a sensitivity analysis with an alternative characterization method was to investigate if the main conclusions of the study would change according to the method used. Impacts esti- mated with the characterization method CML (Guinée et al., 2002) were 16%, 27% y 68% smaller with the economic market, economic total and farmers' perception allocation methods, respectively (average of the 3 impact categories calculated), than without allocation (data not shown in table 4).

24: In general, environmental impacts calculated in this study are low, when compared to other studies from developing countries. The emis- sion intensity of dairy cattle in developing countries ranges from 2 to 9 kg CO2-equivalents/kg fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) (re- viewed by Forabosco et al, 2017) and for industrialized countries carbon footprints were found to be around 1 kg CO2-equivalents/kg ECM or Protein and Fat Corrected Milk (Trydeman Knudsen et al., 2016). Bartl et al. (2011) reported 5.42 kg CO2-equivalents and 14.13 SO2-equivalents for a Peruvian highland system. These differences are mainly due to different feeding strategies (alimentation in the system investigated by Bartl et al. (2011) was based on natural and cultivated pastures) and milk production levels (2.57 kg ECM/cow and day as reported by Bartl et al., 2011).

allocated to milk, ii) some intangible functions cannot be represented with an economic value, iii) the farmers' perception of livestock func- tions does not reflect their economic value and iv) the application of system expansion/substitution to the system studied is limited due to the lack of feasible alternative production systems for some outputs.

The first finding, that the inclusion of non-marketable livestock functions reduces the environmental burden allocated to milk, is in line with other studies. If economic allocation is used and impacts are not only allocated to edible outputs (milk, cheese, beef) but also to other livestock functions, the environmental burden of milk reduces by 9.32%, 20%, 32.73% and 9.64% in the present study and studies by Weiler et al. (2014), Garg et al. (2016) and Kiefer et al. (2015), re- spectively. Allocation according to farmers' perception of functions reduced impacts by 45% and 60% compared to economic allocation to edible outputs in the present study and reported by Weiler et al. (2014), respectively. The results highly depend on the number and types of functions included and the method of choice for economic valuation of the different functions.

The second finding is that social and cultural functions of livestock in smallholder systems are highly valued by livestock keepers and should therefore be considered when allocating environmental impacts in LCA. These types of functions of livestock are often ignored and emphasis is mainly placed on the physical marketed livestock produc- tion (Bettencourt et al. 2015).

The third finding, that farmers' perception of livestock functions does not reflect their economic value shows that farmers' motives for keeping cattle are not only of economic nature. This also means that the application of economic allocation in LCAs of dairy products should be closer evaluated, especially for low output systems. In support of eco- nomic allocation LCA practitioners and scientists argue that it is the generation of economic value which motivates production processes and the model outcomes fairly apportion burdens in relation to benefits (Pelletier et al., 2015, Notarnicola et al., 2017). Ardente and Cellura (2012) argue that economic allocation is able to reflect the intrinsic qualities of co-products and co-services which physical para- meters cannot adequately reflect due to the complexity of production systems. On the other hand, previous studies have also shown im- portant limitations of economic allocation. For example Pelletier and Tyedmers (2011) explain that model outcomes derived with economic allocation might suggest that economically cheap co-products or func- tions are environmentally benign. used in this study to quantify the economic value of these functions accomplish to represents its real value is debatable. Weiler et al. (2014) argue that in the case of intangible functions, economic quantification is unlikely to meaningfully cover all benefits of cattle.

Finally we found that the application of system expansion/sub- stitution to the system studied is limited due to the lack of feasible alternative production systems for some outputs. Despite this limitation we applied system expansion which is an approach suggested by other authors if multifunctionality of agriculture is to be integrated in an assessment (Meier et al., 2015). The results coincide with other studies which also showed a significant reduction of the environmental burden attributed to the main product, compared to allocation approaches (Flysjö et al., 2011; O'Brien et al., 2014; Kiefer et al., 2015). In align with Kiefer et al. (2015) we also found that system expansion/sub- stitution attaches great importance to meat as a coproduct. In our case 70% of subtracted impact is due to the co-production of beef. Previous studies have shown that the results of system expansion/substitution highly depend on the type of meat that is assumed to be replaced (Kristensen et al., 2011; Nguyen et al., 2013b; O'Brien et al., 2014). Nevertheless, the quality of these types of meat is very different and therefore they probably substitute different products but this could not be considered due to the lack of feasible alternative production systems for veal and culled cows. (2015) we conclude that, the decision for the substitution scenario is essential in the system expansion/substitution approach and if there are multiple possible substitution options for the same products that are produced, multiple scenarios are possible, leading each to different results.

6: From the analysis of sensitivity of results to alfalfa ingestion one can conclude that models with an economic impact allocation respond less sensitive to changes in alfalfa ingestion, as an increase of milk yield partially compensates increased emissions. The results of the sensitivity analysis furthermore show that the main objective of the study, which is the comparison of different methodological approaches for handling livestock- multifunctionality in LCA, is not biased by the uncertainty of the amount of alfalfa ingested. The variation of alfalfa ingestion simu- lated does not change the conclusions from a direct comparison of impacts derived by means of the different methods.

Although absolute impact values might vary according to the characterization method used, the final conclusion concerning the comparison of different allocation methods is not be influenced by the choice of the characterization model (CML VS. Recipe).
7: The system investigated is characterized by low input practices and family labor. Official statistics about the production system are rare, out

of date or available only at a bigger scale. Therefore, most information required for modelling had to be obtained through interviews from farmers. But farmers don't manage a written control of production in- puts, and estimating the volume of inputs has been a difficult task for them. Furthermore, in some cases they use measures which are difficult to convert into standard units. The amount of alfalfa ingested by ani- mals for example is quantified by means of the number of times the cow is moved from one alfalfa patch to the next. In contrast, they could provide reliable information about marketable outputs, such as cheese, milk and animals. Another bottleneck is the lack of emission data and emission models adapted to prevailing environmental and agricultural management. Especially a sound estimation of methane emissions from enteric fermentation is crucial, as they are the main greenhouse gas emissions. Methane emissions modelled in the study were 19 g CH4/kg ECM. A recent study reported 13.5 g CH4/kg ECM as average methane emission rate of livestock from US and EU (Niu et al., 2018). Methane measurements from livestock systems in the Peruvian highlands, nevertheless, published far higher emissions (41 g/kg milk) for a high altitude environment (3500 to 4300 m. S. n. m.,) and a low output system (7 L/cow and day; Reyes and Gómez Bravo, 2018). A value between these two emission levels reported in literature is feasible for the study system; nevertheless, a more detailed evaluation of the re- liability of the emission estimated is required but not possible SO far, due to the lack of data from similar systems.

9: <header>5. Conclusions and practical implications of the results </header>

The results of this study showed that the consideration of other li- vestock functions than the production of edible products has a strong impact on the LCA outcome. The results highly depend on the number and types of functions included and the method of choice for economic valuation of the different functions. Economic valuation is possible but not for all functions. Especially social and cultural functions of livestock are difficult or impossible to value in monetary terms. Nevertheless some effort should be undertaken in order to understand them. Also other intangible functions such as the use of cattle as savings asset and risk insurance are of high importance to farmers as farmers' perception allocation method applied in this study showed.

Another finding of the study was that system expansion/substitution attaches great importance to meat as a coproduct. When applying system expansion, the co-product meat should therefore be modelled with special care also considering different meat qualities.

Finally, an important bottleneck in this study was the lack of emission data and emission models adapted to sub-tropical mountain environmental and production settings, as well as the lack of specific life cycle inventories. The development of these models and inventories should be subject to future investigations.
